coredata.dc:title: Design of photoluminescence point-of-care membrane strip for the detection of dopamine
coredata.dc:creator: Babu, Punuri Jayasekhar
Saranya, Sibyala
Raichur, Ashok M.
Doble, Mukesh
coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate the design of point-of-care photoluminescence membrane strip (PoCPMS) by coating the carbon nano dots (CNDs) on polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane for the detection of dopamine (DA). The photoluminescence (PL) of PoCPMS is quenched by treating with Fe2+ solution which is again recovered by adding the dopamine (DA). Thus, providing novel membrane route for the detection of both DA and Fe2+ on single flat form in the presence of UV light (portable pen UV torch). Furthermore, we have demonstrated the PL quenching and recovery phenomenon in aqueous phase.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanomaterials
Photoluminescence
Iron(Fe2+)
Sensors
Dopamine
PVDF membrane

Interaction of different concentrations of dopamine with FeSO4 added CNDs in liquid phase
Qualitative sensing of dopamine using PoCPMS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Absorption (UV–Vis) spectra of CNDs (Inset: CNDs under UV light) and (b) Concentration (10–120 µg/ml) dependent PL emission by CNDs (c) TEM (d) SAED pattern of the CNDs.

(a) PoCPMS showing images of PL quenching (by Fe2+) and its recovery by DA (b) Incapability of various biological molecules as PL recovering agent (c) Graphical representation of PoCPMS showing PL quenching and recovery in presence of UV torch light (d) Formation of CND–Fe complex and DA–Fe complex leading to quenching and recovery of PL respectively.

(a) PL quenching by Fe2+ ions (b) Enlarged view of figure for better clarity (c) Linear plot of PL quenching as a function of Fe2+ concentration (d) Quenching capability of different salts (e) PL enhancing at different concentrations of DA (f) Linear plot of PL recovery as a function of DA concentration.

head.ce:title: Design of photoluminescence point-of-care membrane strip for the detection of dopamine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biomaterials and Bioengineering Research Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Pachhunga University College, Mizoram University (A Central University), Aizawl 796 001, Mizoram, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Bioengineering and Drug Design Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600 036, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Bioengineering and Drug Design Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai 600 036, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Biomaterials and Bioengineering Research Laboratory, Department of Biotechnology, Pachhunga University College, Mizoram University (A Central University), Aizawl-796 001, Mizoram, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate the design of point-of-care photoluminescence membrane strip (PoCPMS) by coating the carbon nano dots (CNDs) on polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane for the detection of dopamine (DA). The photoluminescence (PL) of PoCPMS is quenched by treating with Fe2+ solution which is again recovered by adding the dopamine (DA). Thus, providing novel membrane route for the detection of both DA and Fe2+ on single flat form in the presence of UV light (portable pen UV torch). Furthermore, we have demonstrated the PL quenching and recovery phenomenon in aqueous phase.

ce:section.ce:para: There is a major research focus in addressing health care issues to cater the demands of significantly increasing global population. In current scenario, the requirement of both qualitative and quantitative point-of-care diagnostics in remote and resource inadequate regions are gaining importance. In this connection, nanotechnology based devices in combination with chemistry and biology provides better options in diagnosing health problems with highest possible sensitivity and selectivity at the faster speed.

Laboratory based conventional methods of detecting clinically important metabolites (CIM) such as creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, dopamine, cholesterol, and triglycerides are based on optical markers with absorption wavelength [1]. In recent years, micro RNAs are used as biomarkers for detecting various diseases, especially employed for accurate prognosis of malignancy [2,3]. Nevertheless, these advanced and conventional methods are surpassed by the entry of nanomaterial based devices or protocols in diagnosing CIM. Among many kinds of nanomaterials, carbon nano dots (CNDs) are widely used in various applications especially in detecting CIM due to their inheriting Surface Plasmon Resonance (SPR) based multiple optical changes than the conventional methods [4–6]. We used these CNDs to design point-of-care photoluminescence membrane strip (PoCPMS) for the detection of dopamine (DA).

DA is a neurotransmitter and a clinically important molecule released by the brain that plays a various roles such as memory, emotions, attention, sleep, mood, and learning in human and other animals [7–9]. To the best of our knowledge, design of point-of-care photoluminescence based techniques using CNDs is very limited in case of detecting neurotransmitters, especially dopamine. In this study, we designed a PoCPMS for the detection of dopamine (DA) with high sensitivity and selectivity. PoCPMS is designed by coating CNDs on PVDF membrane. Photoluminescence (PL) of PoCPMS is attributed to CNDs which is quenched and recovered by FeSo4 and DA respectively, thereby detecting both the molecules on single platform.
All the reagents are of analytical grade and obtained from Sigma (Bangalore, India) and Merck (Mumbai, India).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon nano dots (CNDs) and characterization
ce:section.ce:para: 500 ml of Citrullus lanatus pulp was heated in microwave (MW) oven at 1200 W and 2.45 GHz (5 s ON and OFF mode) for 45 min. The resulted solution was centrifuged at 5000 rpm and supernatant comprised of CNDs was collected and freeze dried. Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) was employed to observe the size and shape of synthesized CNDs.

200 µL of synthesized CNDs (1 mg/ml) were drop-cast on an approximately 2.5 diameter polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) membrane and was dried in an oven at 40°C. This process was repeated five times to get bright PL on PVDF. This membrane (PoCPMS) was treated with 0.01 ml of FeSO4 (1 mg/ 100 ml) solution and observed under UV light (365 nm) to monitor the changes in PL intensity.

60 µL of dopamine (1 mg/ml) was added to PoCPMS at a place already treated with 60 µL of FeSO4 (0.01 mg/ml) solution. The PoCPMS was allowed to dry completely and observed for PL behavior of PoCPMS under UV light at 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of FeSO4 and different salts with CNDs in liquid phase
ce:section.ce:para: To find out the quenching capabilities of different salts, 0.1 ml of CNDs (1 mg/ml) are treated with 0.1 ml (1 mg/ 100 ml) of various salt solutions (FeSo4, MgSO4, ZnSO4, Li2SO4, BaSO4, C12H23O2Na, C16H37NO, NH3, Na2SO4, BaSO4, CaSO4, K2SO4, Fe2(SO4)3, ZnSO4, SrSO4, MnSO4, HgSO4.. The PL quenching of CNDs was measured using a multimode micro plate reader (Enspire, USA: Exi 230 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of different concentrations of dopamine with FeSO4 added CNDs in liquid phase
ce:section.ce:para: To the reaction mixture containing 0.1 ml of CNDs (1 mg/ml) and 0.01 ml of iron sulphate (1 mg/100 ml), 0.1 ml of varying concentration of dopamine (1 mg/ml) was added and made up to 1 ml using HEPES buffer. The PL of solution was measured using a multimode micro plate reader. (Please see the supplementary materials S1 for different biomolecules reacting with CND in liquid phase).

The formation of CNDs was confirmed visually in a UV chamber operated at 365 nm by observing the emission of green photoluminescence (PL) (Fig. 1 a, Inset). Furthermore, it was observed that CNDs showed absorption at 280 nm, confirming the formation of CNDs (Fig. 1a). The absorption and fluorescence properties of CNDs were contributed by the functional groups associated with them. The peak observed at 280 ascribed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C–C bonds and a tail at 300 nm is ascribed to the n- π* transition of C–O bonds of carbon core [10]. Also, it was found that the PL intensity of CNDs increases with its concentration (Fig. 1b). CNDs are predominantly spherical in shape which was confirmed by the TEM analysis (Fig. 1c). The semi crystalline nature of the synthesized CNDs are confirmed by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) bright circular rings corresponding to (002) and (101) planes (Fig. 1d).

Carbohydrates of Citrullus lanatus undergo hydrothermal carbonization leads to the formation of CNDs. There is a generation of series of emissive traps due to the presence of various energy level functional groups associated with CNDs. When these CNDs are excited at a certain wavelength, the surfaces of the CNDs are oxidized thereby forming the surface defects which leads to red-shifted emission [6,10].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Qualitative sensing of dopamine using PoCPMS
ce:section.ce:para: PL of PoCPMS was quenched at the area treated with 60 µL of FeSO4 (0.01 mg/mL) solution (Fig. 2 a,c) in the presence of UV light. On other hand, it was observed that the PL was recovered by the addition of DA (60 µL; 1 mg/ml) to the place where it was already quenched by treating with FeSO4. This indicates that DA facilitates in recovering the PL of PoCPMS. Various experiments were conducted with other molecules such as creatinine, bilirubin, glucose, dopamine, glutamic acid, glutathione and thiamine (60 µL; 1 mg/ml) for PL recovery. However, they proved to be incapable of recovering PL of PoCPMS (Fig. 2b).

The possible mechanism for this observation could be that the Fe2+ ions form a CND-Fe complex by attaching with the electron deficit ‘O’ atom present on the CNDs. This leads to structural changes in PL center of the CNDs thereby losses their PL phenomenon [11,12]. On other hand, CNDs (both in liquid and membrane phase) were found to exhibit higher PL on addition of DA. This is due to significantly increased oxidation of Fe(II) with increase of DA concentration [13]. The movement DA added to Fe(II) containing solution, it could interact with Fe(II) and undergoes oxido-reduction reaction thereby forming the DA–Fe complex. During this reaction, Fe(II) gets oxidised to form Fe (III) and DA gets reduced [13]. The formed Fe (III) doesn’t show any significant quenching activity as reported earlier [14]. Thus, the DA–Fe complex facilitates CNDs to exhibit their inherited PL property thereby recovering the PL on PoCPMS. Additionally, DA molecules interact with CNDs and forms CND-DA complex which may be responsible for recovery of PL [15].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative sensing of dopamine and iron
ce:section.ce:para: Similar phenomenon as obtained on the PoCPMS was observed in liquid phase sensing of dopamine and Fe2+. The PL of CNDs intensity significantly decreased with increasing concentration of Fe(II) (0.1–2 µg/ml) (Fig. 3 a & b for close observation of peaks). The relative change of the fluorescence intensity versus the initial intensity (F0/F) at 230 nm as a function of Fe concentration ([Fe]) can be monitored by the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 3c). The lower limit of detection (LOD) is found to be as low as 0.0002 µg/ml. PL of CNDs is strongly quenched by Fe2+ among many salts tested in this study (Fig. 3d). Fe2+ ion breaks the unsaturated C=O bonds present in ketone, carboxylic acid and aldehyde functional groups present on the surface of CNDs. Then, the Fe2+ atom forms a bond with electron deficit oxygen atom associated with CNDs thereby forming a Fe-CNDs complex. Due to this complex formation, structural changes occurs in PL center of CNDs leads to the loss of PL phenomenon [11,12,16]. On other hand, it is seen that substantial recovery in PL intensity is observed after addition of DA to a solution comprising of Fe2+ and CNDs (Fig. 3e). The corresponding PL recovery plot at different concentrations of DA is shown in Fig. 3f. The purpose of carrying out these experiments was to find out the lowest concentration of DA where it will recover the maximum PL and was found that 60 µg/ml of DA served the purpose. Also, different molecules tested for PL recovery along with DA showed insignificant PL recovery of CNDs (Supplementary material S1). The present method of detecting DA found to be a superior among many other methods reported earlier (Supplementary material T1).

The PoCPMS was developed for the simultaneous selective detection of both Fe(II) and DA on single flat form. The detection method involves the PL quenching of PoCPMS in the presence of Fe(II) and the recovery of PL in the presence of DA. Additionally, the current work demonstrates the detection of both analyte (Fe2+ and DA) in liquid phase, thus providing the new PL based route for the detection of DA.
This work was filed for patent (201941043232).

Punuri Jayasekhar Babu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Sibyala Saranya: Investigation, Methodology, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Askok M Raichur: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Mukesh Doble: Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Punuri J Babu would like to acknowledge IIT for the infrastructure provided to carry out his research and UGC, Govt. of India for providing research funds vide Project No: PDFSS-2014-2015-SC-AND-4750 and CSIR for fellowship under pool scientist’s scheme.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2020.128316.
